summary: Patrick McKenzie reflects on his first two years at Stripe, explaining why he chose to work there instead of running his own business. He frames the decision around leverage — Stripe's scale allows his work to impact far more businesses than he could reach independently. He describes his unusual role as a senior individual contributor, his work on Stripe Atlas, security red-teaming, internal writing, and mentoring. The post also covers the challenges of hypergrowth, remote work from Japan, and how working at a large organization changed his views on ambition and impact.
key_points: Leverage is the core reason to join a larger organization: Stripe's scale means individual decisions can have outsized impact across the entire portfolio of businesses on the platform

Being a senior individual contributor means spending increasing time on metawork — culture, strategy, mentoring — rather than just 'the work' itself, and companies should have explicit IC career tracks

Hypergrowth breaks intuitive understanding of organizations: at a company doubling annually, half your coworkers always have less than a year of experience, and material impact thresholds constantly shift

Working at scale corrected McKenzie's views on ambition — properly harnessed ambition is enormously productive, and the culture of mocking big visions discourages scrupulous people from attempting extraordinary things
Internal tooling and composability are massively underinvested in at most companies; each additional tool makes wide swathes of operations more valuable in unanticipated ways
money_quotes: Stripe is a celebration of the written word which happens to be incorporated in the state of Delaware.

Mediocrity doesn't happen because a villain kills good work then cackles maniacally. BigCo doesn't set out to be mediocre… BigCo just has an awful lot of people with an awful lot on their plate, and many of them are incentivized to make locally-optimal decisions at the expense of competing global goals.

There is a great, great difference between 'Build a credit card processor? That's impossible.' and 'Build a credit card processor? That probably involves compliance with an enumerable set of regulations and writing a finite number of lines of code.'
I've also had some time to crawl around the ducts of Internet commerce, which is a beautiful fractal described by an equation capable of summoning horrors from beyond spacetime.
If you think of it as less a Strategy Memo and more a pull request for a very complicated program which directs the activities of dozens or hundreds of people, it gets a lot easier to stomach.

Sometimes the reason for an outrageous result isn't a principal/agent problem or 'Maximize the evil' or incompetence, it is that writing a dashboard which would have surfaced the problem in time to fix it was beyond the SQL capabilities of the most directly responsible individual.
Leadership is just programming for organizations.
The route to great work is mostly hire small teams of very talented people who very intently care about something and incentivize great work amongst numerous other competing worthy priorities.
key_insight: Joining a high-growth company trading autonomy for leverage can multiply your impact far beyond what solo entrepreneurship allows, but it requires accepting organizational overhead and the constant recalibration that hypergrowth demands.
